title: What Is Money, and Why Do We Use It?

comps.text: The Day Money Vanished

comps.alt: School canteen with an empty cash counter

comps.text: Imagine you walk into your canteen tomorrow and every rupee on Earth has vanished. You are hungry. All you have in your bag is three mangoes.
No cash. No UPI. No cards. If you want that samosa, you have to convince the canteen uncle to take your mangoes instead.
Sounds simple? It is not. And the reason why is exactly why humans invented money.
Try Trading Without Money
Four friends each have something to trade, and each wants something else. Match every person to the friend who has exactly what they want.

comps.instruction: Match each person to the friend who has what they want.

left.name: Kabir
left.sub: has a notebook, wants a pencil box

left.name: Diya
left.sub: has a pencil box, wants mangoes

comps.okFb: You did it, all four trades worked!
comps.badFb: Not quite. Look at what each person wants, then find who is holding it.

comps.text: You solved it. But notice something. This only worked because all four wanted exactly what the other person had. In real life that almost never happens. Imagine finding a perfect match for every single thing you buy.
One Simple Idea Changed Everything
People realised they did not need a perfect match. They just needed one thing that everybody agreed to accept. Then anyone could trade with anyone.

comps.b: Instead of mangoes to samosa, you go mangoes to money to samosa. Sell what you have to anyone. Buy what you want from anyone. No perfect match needed.

comps.text: Money is not wealth itself. It is a tool that makes exchanging value easy.
That is it. That is the whole invention. Money is just something everyone agrees to accept, so nobody has to hunt for a perfect trade ever again.
Money Does Three Jobs

cards.b: Everyone accepts it, so you can buy from anyone.
Mangoes rot in a week. 100 rupees still buys something next month. Money lets you save value for later.
A price tag lets you compare a 40 rupee samosa with a 4,000 rupee pair of shoes instantly.

comps.q: Riya sells her old cycle for 2,000 rupees and keeps the money for six months to buy a laptop. Which job of money is she leaning on most?

options.fb: Close. She did use money to sell the cycle. But the important part here is that she is holding it for six months.
Exactly. She is parking value for later, that is money's storage job. A cycle would rust, the 2,000 rupees waits patiently.
Comparing is about price tags. Riya is not comparing anything, she is waiting.
Money is definitely doing something here. Look at what happens during those six months.

comps.text: Three jobs. Remember them, almost every money decision you will ever make uses at least one.
Count How Often Money Touches Your Day
You might think you barely use money. Tap each moment in this school day and see what is really going on.

points.label: Bus ride
points.text: 15 rupees fare. Someone paid for the bus, the fuel, and the driver's salary, all with money.

points.label: Canteen samosa
points.text: 40 rupees. The canteen used your money to buy flour, potatoes and oil from someone else.

points.label: New pen
points.text: 20 rupees. That pen crossed a factory, a truck and a shop. Money moved at every step.

points.label: Dinner at home
points.text: Groceries, gas, electricity. Money quietly paid for all of it before you sat down.

points.label: Game app
points.text: Even free apps earn money from ads. Nothing is truly free.

comps.title: A typical student's week in rupees

comps.text: Money touched your day at least ten times before you even noticed. That is how good a tool it is, it disappears into the background.
So What Is Currency?
Every country issues its own official money. That official money is called its currency. In India our currency is the Indian Rupee, issued by the Reserve Bank of India.

comps.alt: World map with rupee, dollar, yen and euro

comps.instruction: Tap a currency, then tap its home country.
comps.okFb: Spot on. Same idea, different name and symbol.
comps.badFb: Check the symbol carefully. The rupee sign is used only in India.

comps.text: Different countries, different currencies, but every single one does the same three jobs.
Travel anywhere and the notes look different. What they do never changes.
Pause and Think
No right answer here. I am just curious about you.

comps.prompt: Think of the last thing you bought. If money did not exist, what would you have had to trade to get it, and would that trade have actually worked?

comps.text: Most learners realise the trade would have failed. That tiny realisation is the whole point of this lesson.
Challenge: One Hour Without Money
At the school fair, money is banned for one hour. You have a football. You want a plate of chaat. The chaat stall owner does not want a football, he wants a phone charger.

comps.instruction: Put these steps in the order that actually gets you the chaat.

cards.text: Find someone who wants a football
Check if they have a phone charger
Trade the football for the charger

comps.okFb: Four steps, and you needed to get lucky twice.
comps.badFb: Work backwards. What must you be holding right before you get the chaat?

comps.text: Four steps. Two lucky matches. With money? One step: pay 40 rupees. That is why we use it.

comps.sentence: Money works because everyone ______ that it will be accepted.
comps.hint: It starts with the letter t
comps.okFb: Trust. That is the invisible thing holding the whole system up.
comps.badFb: Think about why the canteen uncle takes a paper note instead of mangoes.

comps.title: Lesson Complete!
comps.subtitle: What Is Money, and Why Do We Use It?

takeaways.b: Without it, every trade needs two people who want exactly what the other has.
Gets you things. Waits for you. Compares things.

takeaways.h: Currency is a country's official money
takeaways.b: Rupee in India, dollar in the USA. Different names, same three jobs.

comps.badgeCaption: You figured out what money really is.
comps.achievement: First lesson of your PiggyPath journey complete. 1 of 5 in this level.
comps.teaser: Next time I will show you what people actually used as money before coins existed. Salt. Shells. Even cattle. Some of it gets weird.
